perturbation.original_text: If Developer becomes legally compelled to disclose any Sample Data, Developer shall provide FDI with prompt prior written notice of such requirement so that FDI may seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy, or both, or waive compliance with the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: If Developer becomes legally compelled to disclose any Sample Data, Developer shall notify FDI.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the portion about FDI seeking a protective order or waiving compliance may violate laws pertaining to trade secrets and confidential information, as it eliminates the option for FDI to protect its data through legal means. New York law recognizes the importance of protective orders in safeguarding trade secrets.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) § 3103(a)
perturbation.law_explanation: CPLR § 3103(a) allows courts to issue protective orders to prevent abuse or unreasonable annoyance, prejudice, or disadvantage during the disclosure process. By removing the clause that allows FDI to seek a protective order, the modified text potentially hinders FDI's ability to protect its confidential information as is permitted under New York law.

Protective orders. (a) Prevention of abuse. The court may at any time on its own initiative, or on motion of any party or of any person from whom or about whom discovery is sought, make a protective order denying, limiting, conditioning or regulating the use of any disclosure device. Such order shall be designed to prevent unreasonable annoyance, expense, embarrassment, disadvantage, or other prejudice to any person or the courts. (b) Suspension of disclosure pending application for protective order. Service of a notice of motion for a protective order shall suspend disclosure of the particular matter in dispute. (c) Suppression of information improperly obtained. If any disclosure under this article has been improperly or irregularly obtained so that a substantial right of a party is prejudiced, the court, on motion, may make an appropriate order, including an order that the information be suppressed.
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes a crucial component of the original agreement that allowed FDI to seek a protective order, which is explicitly permitted under CPLR § 3103(a) as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1. The original text ensured FDI could take legal action to protect its confidential information before disclosure, whereas the changed text only requires notification, potentially undermining FDI's ability to safeguard its trade secrets as intended by law.

perturbation.original_text: Developer shall have each employee to whom any Sample Data will be disclosed, or who will have access to Sample Data, sign a nondisclosure agreement that is substantially similar to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Developer may have each employee to whom any Sample Data will be disclosed, or who will have access to Sample Data, sign a nondisclosure agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall' to 'may' weakens the requirement for employees to sign an NDA. This could be a violation of New York trade secret law, as reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy are required, including employee agreements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Uniform Trade Secrets Act (NYUTSA), Article 24-A of the General Business Law § 350
perturbation.law_explanation: NYUTSA requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. While not explicitly mandating employee NDAs, failing to ensure employees are bound by confidentiality agreements can be interpreted as a failure to take reasonable measures, thereby weakening trade secret protection under New York Law.  Changing 'shall' to 'may' weakens the level of protection.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall' to 'may' weakens the obligation for employees to sign a nondisclosure agreement. The Law Explanation states that under NYUTSA, reasonable measures must be taken to protect trade secrets. While the scraped law snippet does not directly mention trade secrets or NDA requirements, the Law Explanation argues that not ensuring employees are bound by confidentiality can be seen as a failure to take reasonable measures. The change from 'shall' (mandatory) to 'may' (permissive) directly reduces the level of protection afforded to the data, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Developer shall take all reasonable measures to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure or use of the Sample Data, including, without limitation, using the highest degree of care that Developer uses to protect its own confidential information.
perturbation.changed_text: Developer shall take measures to protect the Sample Data.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'all reasonable measures' and the 'highest degree of care' standard weakens the protection afforded to the confidential information, potentially violating New York trade secret law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Uniform Trade Secrets Act (NYUTSA), Article 24-A of the General Business Law § 350
perturbation.law_explanation: NYUTSA requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets.  Reducing the standard of care and removing the explicit obligation to use 'all reasonable measures' can be seen as failing to provide adequate protection, weakening the legal basis for trade secret protection in New York.
perturbation.justification: The original text requires the 'highest degree of care' and 'all reasonable measures' to protect Sample Data, aligning with the NYUTSA's expectation of reasonable measures to safeguard trade secrets. The changed text removes these specific requirements, weakening the obligation to protect the data, creating a legal contradiction. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions 'Fair Trade Law' but lacks specific details on trade secret protection, however, the Perturbation and Law explanations indicate this reduction in protection could fail to meet the standard of care required to maintain trade secret protection under New York law.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that FDI deems it appropriate to initiate any action to enforce the obligations of Developer under this Agreement, and should Developer be held to have breached this Agreement, Developer shall reimburse FDI for all costs and expenses, including reasonable attorneys’ fees.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that FDI deems it appropriate to initiate any action to enforce the obligations of Developer under this Agreement, Developer may reimburse FDI for costs and expenses.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall reimburse' to 'may reimburse' removes the guarantee of reimbursement for legal costs if the Developer breaches the agreement. This contradicts New York law, as it limits the remedies available to the plaintiff (FDI) in the event of a breach of contract, and New York law allows for the recovery of attorney's fees when explicitly agreed upon in a contract and a party breaches that contract.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) § 3001
perturbation.law_explanation: CPLR § 3001 provides the court the power to declare the rights and other legal relations on request for such declaration whether or not further relief is or could be claimed. This section read in conjunction with other contract laws and rulings, would permit FDI to claim attorney fees as provided in the contract. By weakening the obligation to reimburse, the contract contradicts a fundamental legal principle of contract enforcement under New York law.

Declaratory judgment. The supreme court may render a declaratory judgment having the effect of a final judgment as to the rights and other legal relations of the parties to a justiciable controversy whether or not further relief is or could be claimed. If the court declines to render such a judgment it shall state its grounds. A party who has brought a claim for personal injury or wrongful death against another party may maintain a declaratory judgment action directly against the insurer of such other party, as provided in paragraph six of subsection (a) of section three thousand four hundred twenty of the insurance law.
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates reimbursement of legal costs to FDI if the Developer breaches the agreement, using 'shall reimburse'. The changed text alters this to 'may reimburse', making reimbursement discretionary. The Perturbation Explanation states that this change removes the guarantee of reimbursement, contradicting New York law because it limits remedies available to FDI in case of a breach, when New York law allows for attorney's fees when explicitly agreed upon in a contract and a party breaches that contract. The Law Explanation mentions CPLR § 3001, which permits FDI to claim attorney fees as provided in the contract, supporting the argument that weakening the reimbursement obligation contradicts contract enforcement principles under New York law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 includes SECTION 3001 Declaratory judgment, reinforcing the court's power to declare rights and legal relations, which can include the right to claim attorney fees as originally stipulated in the contract. The change from 'shall' to 'may' significantly undermines the original guarantee, creating a legal contradiction.
